title: Project Proposal
text:
Financial technology (fintech) has become a major driver of financial inclusion and digital transformation, particularly in emerging markets where digital platforms expand access to financial services. Despite the rapid growth of fintech ecosystem, real transactional behavior, product usage and customer engagement and financial activity remain limited. 
The Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics Dataset addresses this gap by providing a comprehensive dataset covering 48,723 Jan and Dec 2023. This dataset captures detailed customer financial behaviors including transaction activity, product usage, engagement levels and customer tenure within a fintech platform. 
By analyzing this dataset, valuable insights can be obtained regarding how customers interact with digital financial services, how their behavior evolves over time and which factors contribute most significantly to long-term customer value. 
This dashboard presents a data-driven analysis that combines Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), customer segmentation (Clustering) and predictive modeling techniques to better understand fintech customer behavior and support strategic decision-making. 



The primary objective of this project is to analyze customer financial behavior and predict long-term customer value using the dataset. Specifically, the project aims to identify key behavior and transactional factors that influence customer lifetime value, develop predictive models for future financial value generated by each customer and segment customers into meaningful behavioral groups to understand different engagement and product usage patterns. 



Understanding customer financial behavior is critical for fintech platforms aiming to maximize long-term profitability and improve customer experience. Traditional approaches often treat customers as a homogeneous group, overlooking the diverse behavioral patterns that influence customer value. By combining predictive modeling with custering analysis, this project provides a more comprehensive perspective on customer engagement.  



This section highlights the potential challenges that the team might face. It also outlines the mitigation plans that will be implemented to reduce the risks associated with the current Shiny app project. 








Potential Challenges 
Details 
Mitigation Plan 


Inconsistent data preparation 
Different ways of data cleaning. 
To identify critical data preparation steps that require strict standardization. 


Data size 
The total file size amounts to ~130MB and the app might be computationally heavy to run. 
Precomputation of summaries to reduce on the need for backend calculation. 


Consistency of modules 
All the modules should be aligned in the business story they are trying to tell. They should have a natural flow. 
Need to firm up the agreed business question. 


Over cluttering of charts 
Multiple charts that repeats the same message 
To be highly selective on the charts to tell the story. 


Potential empty plots due to too much filtering 
Having too much filter resulting in blank plots 
To select only relevant features for slicing 


Conflicting of Named variable 
In the respective modules the team member might be using the same naming convention for the data view. 
To have a standardized naming convention (i.e. unique prefix to be add to avoid conflict). 






Team Workflow: The team will divide the work based on the three main modules of the application, with each member taking responsibility for one analytical component. 
Data Preparation: To ensure consistency across the project, the team will maintain a shared preprocessed base dataset with a common data structure, standardised variable definitions, and consistent coding conventions. This will support the smooth integration of all modules into a unified Shiny application. In addition, regular discussions and testing sessions will be conducted to ensure alignment across the EDA, clustering, and predictive modelling components. 
Module Integration: The team will also hold discussions to determine the most relevant visualizations and analytical outputs that can link the three modules in a coherent manner, thereby enhancing the usability and overall value of the Shiny application for end users. 



The following table details the scope of work and responsibilities assigned to the respective members of the Team. The Team has also included a timeline to aid the Project’s internal progress monitoring.





Our prototype ideation started with low-fidelity sketches to outline the layout, navigation, and key functionalities of the Shiny application. The sketches were then refined into a higher-fidelity prototype that improves visual structure and usability.
Ideation Sketches


High-fidelity Prototype

Key Features

Side navigation tabs: allow user to select and move around the major modules of our app
Parameters selection: consist of variables lists or sliders to let user customize the parameters for plotting
Plotting area: show the plots and other components such as statistic metrics tab




The expected outcome of the project is a functional and interactive R Shiny dashboard that enables users to configure models, run analyses, and interpret results within a single visual analytics environment. The final application is expected to support analytical storytelling and practical decision making by combining descriptive, unsupervised, and supervised analytics in one platform.

title: ISSS608 Group 4 Project Meeting Minutes
text:
Date: 14/03/2026
Location: SMU Campus
Attendees: Son, Edward, Ho



The team discussed on the need to standardize base data
Ho shared that he have dropped only one columns due to having multiple missing value. There is no other concern with the rest of the team.





Son shared on the initial set up of website using Quarto and load in the proposal as a draft





The team further aligned on the job scope after meeting with Prof Kam
Edward suggested to stick with separating to task by modules:

Son will focus on the Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) module.



Edward will handle the Clustering module.



Ho will be responsible for the Predictive Modelling module.

title: ISSS608 Group 4 Project Meeting Minutes
text:
Project Meeting 2
Date: 15/03/2026
Location: Microsoft Teams Online Call
Attendees: Son, Edward, Ho

1. Project Coordination and Planning

The team discussed arranging a face-to-face meetup session – planned to last approximately four hours. This session aims to facilitate deeper collaboration and alignment across all project components.
Additionally, the team shared and reviewed the project storyboard to ensure consistency in design and direction before further development.



2. Development Workflow and Deployment Strategy

Ho & Ed raised concerns regarding Netlify limitations, particularly on excessive republishing.
The team agreed to focus on developing modules locally and minimise unnecessary redeployments.



3. Storyboard and Proposal Updates

Son was assigned to clean up the storyboard and update the project proposal accordingly, scheduled to be completed by 18 March 2026 to ensure alignment with project requirements.



4. Modules Development
The team outlined several key features to be implemented in the EDA module:

Visualisation of data distributions
Toggle functionality for different data types (bivariate and multivariate analysis)
User selection of dependent and output variables
Smart EDA functionalities to enhance automated insights

For the Clustering & Prediction modules:

Interactive correlation matrix with user input controls
Analysis of customer lifetime value (CLV) as the target variable, including chart setup
User-controlled toggles for outlier removal
Integration of data transformation steps with visual feedback

These tasks are collaboratively assigned among Son, Ho, and Edward.

title: ISSS608 Group 4 Project Meeting Minutes
text:
Project Meeting 3
Date: 20/03/2026
Location: SMU Campus
Attendees: Son, Edward, Ho
The team focused on the foundational setup of the Shiny application, specifically addressing the UI framework and data clustering logic.

1. Application Logic & Clustering

User Flow: The app will allow users to select specific features/variables to perform clustering.



Forecasting: Once clusters are generated, the app will map these results and perform a group_by function to provide aggregate forecasting for each individual cluster.
Simplification: The team agreed to avoid “over-complicating” the prototype. The initial focus will be on basic data understanding before adding advanced features like export functions.



2. UI/UX Design

Framework: Use of bs4dash was confirmed as it is more user-friendly and requires less manual configuration than the “basic” page layout.
Layout: The interface will feature a sidebar navigation tab, side panel for user inputs (dropdowns, sliders, file uploads) and a main panel for visual outputs (histograms/charts).
Fidelity: The team discussed using “low-fidelity” wireframes from previous phase to move toward “high-fidelity” design.



3. Deployment & Version Control

Repository Strategy: to avoid rendering errors, the team decided to use two separate GitHub repositories: one for the main website and one for the Shiny app.
Integration: The main website will include a redirect link pointing directly to the hosted Shiny application.
Access: Son added Ho and Ed as collaborators on the new repositories.



4. Action Items after Meetup

Edward to finalize the GitHub pull/push connection and verify the redirect link on the main website.
Ho to begin building the app’s basic bs4dash framework.
Son begin to draft the “User Guide”.
Each members to continue the development of their respective modules.
The team agreed to meet in person again on Saturday 28 March to review and finalize the prototype & modules.

title: ISSS608 Group 4 Project Meeting Minutes
text:
Project Meeting 4
Date: 28/03/2026
Location: SMU Campus
Attendees: Son, Edward, Ho
Theme: Fine-tuning EDA, Clustering Logic, and Poster Logistics

1. App Performance & Data Handling
The team discussed optimizing the user experience by limiting data overhead and improving initial data inspection.

Upload Limits: Ho proposed limiting users to a maximum of three version uploads to prevent hitting system limits.
Data Glimpse: to speed up the process, the team agreed on using a sampling approach. Instead of loading the full dataset for every check, the app will show a “glimpse” or a small portion to ensure the model fits before full execution.
Data Cleaning: The “Active Data” framework was confirmed. All modules must refer to the active data object to maintain consistency across cleaning, EDA, and modeling.



2. EDA Customization
The team debated on how to make the EDA more interactive and meaningful for end-users.

Variable Handling: Son suggested removing non-analytical fields like CustomerID from univariate plots, but Ed proposed keeping them as “unit counts” (using distinct_count) to track unique customers across categories like Gender or Occupation.
The “French Fry” Histogram: A key discussion point was allowing users to toggle bin sizes.

Small bins = “French Fries” (High detail/narrow distribution).
Large bins = “Hash Browns” (Collapsed/generalized distribution).

Log Scaling: For transaction values that are highly skewed, Ho and Ed agreed to implement Log Transformation options to compress the scale and get better visualizations.



3. Clustering Logic & Optimal ‘K’
Edward walked the team through the clustering module, emphasizing the need for both automated and subjective analysis.

Feature Selection: The app will allow users to select features and check for multicollinearity (using methodologies taught by Prof Kam) before running the simulation.
Determining Clusters: The team will use two primary metrics:

Elbow Plot: To see where the “cost” of adding another cluster drops off.
Silhouette Score (referred to as “Silvia Score”): Where the higher the score, the better the cluster separation.

Note on Subjectivity: Ed noted that the Elbow plot is subjective. While an algorithm can suggest a path, the user should be allowed to choose the cluster count that makes the most sense for their business logic.



4. GitHub Hygiene & Publishing
The team shared some “lessons learned” to prevent project crashes.

Commit First: Ed & Son reminded everyone: Always commit your changes before pulling.
Publishing Protocol: Do not close the RStudio terminal prematurely while the app is “Republishing.” Closing it halfway leads to a 30-minute troubleshooting session (or requires diving into Git Bash).



5. Poster & Deadline
Son will lead the design of the A1-sized poster on Figma.

Requirement: Every team member needs to provide:

A screenshot of their respective module.
A brief summary/description of what the module does.

The “A Chicken Rice Story” Name: Son expressed doubt about the project name, but the team decided to stick with it – joking that the ultimate goal of working/studying is “to eat chicken rice every day.”
Later the team agreed with the new project name “ChickInsights” - a combination of “Chicken Thigh” and “Insights”



6. Next Action Items after Meeting

Son to complete Univariate/Bivariate customization.
Edward to finalize Clustering Module & Export function.
Ho to finalize Predictive Model Comparison charts.
The team agreed to arrange the last Teams Online Call on Tuesday 31 March for Poster Review and Finalization of Shiny App.
